Hey, JB here, the Wolf of Wall Street. Time for your weekly intensive and it's going to be an awesome one. As promised, what I'm going to do today is I'm going to actually deconstruct my own sale for you. That sale that I did live, right? You saw me clothing some guy on from a dairy company, whatever it was, right? Well, there's a whole story behind that sale. That sale, I should say. I still can't live my own sale, right? And I'll tell you this story because it's really important to put the whole thing into context. And there's so much to be learned by this. And I really think that, you know, the main lesson you're going to see here is what it really means to stay on the straight line in the real world. In other words, you know, there's the syntax to the straight line, right? Which is your perfect sale. You want to go from the open to the close. And in the real world, though, it's not always so cut and dry, right? You know, sometimes, you know, you're being taken off the line and also every sales a little bit. Instead, has its own unique style to it, right? So you'll watch here how I sort of managed to sort of jump around a little bit here and there. But I'm always right on the key is I'm always in those healthy boundaries. That's the key. I never go outside the boundaries. And in addition to that, one of the key points, and for those of you who are on the daily motivations, you already know, I already give you a just a quick some thoughts on this sale is that one thing I learned from watching my own sale, partaking it, you know, I already looked at it before, before I did this training, right? Is that control is not an absolute meaning that you and I say take immediate control of the sale. Well, the reality is that yes, you need to take control, but that control continues to evolve as you move down the straight line as you go forward in the sale. And if you're doing things right, that controls getting firmer and firmer and firmer. And if you're doing things wrong, it's weakening and weakening and weakening. In other words, I've said to you in the past, I said, you know, guys, I said, yes, you take immediate control of the sale. It's a gut reaction in the first two seconds, even quicker in person. I said, and then you need to make sure that the things that you say after like, you know, 15 seconds later, you say stupid shit. Well, what will happen is their conscious mind now will override their unconscious mind, which created this perception, you know, their unconscious mind said, wow, this guy's an expert. It was based on a pattern they have. So he hit the right pattern. They proceed you as being an expert in your field shop on the ball, right? But if you say stupid shit, they're like, oops, okay, the guy tried to fool me. He's not an expert. So I've said that to you before, but what I've never really explained in detail here, which is such a critical point, is that it's even more than that. It goes deeper. In other words, when you take control, that controls almost very much like rapport. And since that rapport is not a constant, controls not a constant. It's always either, you know, firmening, getting more firm or weakening based on the last things that you said, based on how sharp you seem. All right. And with that, let's rock and roll. All right. Here's the story. So about, I would say 10 years ago, nine years ago, I was approached by a company in Australia that were their occupational psychologists. And what they really do is they specialize in using psychology to enhance performance in the workplace. And they work with a lot of blue chip companies, mostly in Australia, but some worldwide. And they approached me and said, listen, what we want to do here is we'd like to essentially test you and find out what it is about you, what's your DNA for sales, so to speak, that makes you so effective. Why have you always broke the records wherever you go? And also we want to understand the straight line system, break that down. And we'd like to then turn that into a product that we can then sell to these large corporations. It'll be invaluable, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. And I said, well, you got to pay me for that. And they did pay me a handsome amount of money. Unfortunately, they paid me too much. They actually believe it or not, they ran out of money. They paid me everything they had. Idiots. I wouldn't have wanted that, but they did. And they never really launched it because they just paid me everything they get done, the actual filming. Crazy story. Anyway, they put me through a whole battery of tests. Everything with the fucking stupid Rorschach ink plots to answering those stupid disk evaluations that you do where you're more than this, less than that. They did all this shit to me, right? What's your favorite color? What's this? What are your favorite tendencies? What do you hate? What do you love? All this stuff, right? And part of it, though, and I think probably the most telling was what they did was called critical incident scenarios, meaning they actually set up mock sales. So what they did is they handed me a dossier and that's what this is. This is a mock sale. So they said, listen, we're going to give you, here's this information. It was like about, yeah, thick, right? About 20, 30 pages of information about the dairy industry, about a fictitious dairy company in Australia, about the general trends in the industry, about their goals to move into China, about their successes in Australia, their struggles in Australia. Basically all the things they wanted to do, they even set up a fictitious referral. I got it from someone when they married was a referral, right? And I was this sort of larger investment or merchant style bank and they were dealing with a small mom and pop bank and my goal was to simply open up an account with them. That was the goal, right? And when they handed me this dossier, they said, listen, you have 15 minutes to go through this thing and then when you're done, we're going to film this whole thing and we'll see if you can close the person. Now, two interesting things happened here. Number one is that I was not told this, but the person who played the CEO did a great job, by the way. He was actually an actor. He was an actor and he was told by this company that under no circumstances whatsoever, was he to say yes. I was not going to close this guy. I was going to be unclosable, all right? How compelling my pitch was, he would say, nope, nope, nope. I wasn't going to be able to close the guy, right? That was the deal, all right? Of course, I ended up closing him anyway, which is pretty funny, right? And that's a, we'll get to that punch line in the end. You'll see why he closed, even if he was told not to. That was the first thing. The second thing was this aspect of the 15 minutes. They said to me, you know, you only have 15 minutes. So I said, all right, fair enough. So they, you know, they left the room and I sat there and I started scribbling out my notes and what I was looking to do was write a pitch. I was looking for my key language patterns. I was looking for, you know, how was I going to explain his industry? What was going to be my hook essentially? What was going to be my compelling logical benefits, my emotional benefits? I was looking for all the key things that I would need to write a perfect straight line script. And obviously you need a lot more than 15 minutes to do that, right? So about 15 minutes later, they walked back in and I'm still scribbling away. And they say to me, oh, are you still working? Yeah, yeah, I said, oh, they said take as much time as you want. I'm like, oh, great, thanks a lot. I'll let you know what I'm done. So I kept writing and I kept writing. I really started really digging into this information. About 30 more minutes later, they come back to me. They're like, are you ready now? I said, well, I need a little more time. I said, I'll take as much time as you want. And I wrote for another 30 minutes. I spent all told that they came back three or four times. I spent about two hours, two hours preparing for this presentation. All right. And after I had prepared and I really understood what my strategy was going to be, I found a way in. I thought it would be the most logical way to close this person. The guy, the actor walked in the door. I very quickly memorized core language patterns. And I just was, you know, using my flexibility here to do the best I could in this short period of time. But it's just that I have a lot of experiences of business person. I always say that when you act as if the key to success to acting as if, as you got to close the knowledge gap, in other words, you act like an expert and you're going to become an expert. So even if you just get installed, I want you to commit to becoming like it to expert. So you sound like this when you get in first one in the beginning, you're right all out, but there's no reason you shouldn't be studying your ass off and closing that knowledge gap very quickly. It's why I had that guy Bobby Kosh. I had the one kid that kind of didn't get it. He was like, you know, the sort of the lower IQ of Stratton, right? And Bobby struggled in the beginning. I made him walk around the Wall Street Journal every day and really cover to cover. I wanted him to understand basic business concepts to speak the lingo. Very important. Watch what I do here. Do you see the, did you keep, look at this and watch the active listening and notice how I kept using little bits of industry information to reinforce my positioning as an expert. And, and what else do you notice then? No matter how many times he said, let me think of it. Did I ever seem flustered or lose my cool? No, I'm a machine. I'm like, I'm like, I'm like five loops. I ran five loops on this guy and did it ever seem like I was even pressuring him? It didn't. He was giving me by signals the whole way through because I had earned the right to ask him for the order because I gave him relevant information. I was solving his problems. Doesn't matter what industry you're in. You could always take the same strategy and remember the key is inside the boundaries. That's what makes this sale work so well. So yes, I made mistakes. No doubt. It's the first ever pictures fictitious product. I made errors. I would do it differently. I probably have my, my questions scripted out a little bit more coherently. I probably wouldn't open. I probably would have handled a little bit different. I would have had a more clear transition between the essentially intelligence gathering and the main body. But what happened was I was, because I'm so experienced, I was always doing them all at the same time. And I just nailed it. And you notice when we got to the back half of the sale, that's when it really took on the pure element of a straight line sale. In the back half, it was like just pure looping, looping, looping, looping. And every time I kept looping, he kept hopping to a new objection, but it was weaker objections and weaker objection was obviously bullshit. And at the end, what they, the reason he said yes, he said yes, he said yes. The reason he said yes, and he is the most important thing is this is truly what it means to box someone in. It's not evil. Boxing someone in means you give them no conceivable reason to not say yes. How could this guy say no after all I'd said and done? He had to be kind of an asshole. Like I, I had proved by some, I had created such a perfect case.
